sources.notes: Statement and constraints: at most k blue-paint segments, minimize the maximum penalty of wrong cells.
Official editorial solution binary-searches the allowed penalty and greedily counts required blue segments among cells with penalty above the threshold.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Choose at most k intervals to repaint blue on an initially red strip, minimizing the maximum penalty among cells whose final color is wrong.
annotation.constraints: Total n over tests is at most 3e5 and penalties go up to 1e9, so the answer can be binary searched with a linear feasibility check.
annotation.core_idea: For a candidate penalty x, cells with a_i <= x may be ignored. Among important cells with a_i > x, red cells are hard barriers and blue cells must be covered. The number of required operations is exactly the number of contiguous important-blue groups separated by important-red cells; x is feasible iff this count is at most k.
annotation.complexity: O(n log A) per test batch, where A is the penalty range.
tricks: Turn minimization of maximum wrong penalty into a threshold feasibility problem.
Ignore low-penalty cells and count required high-penalty blue groups.

solution_variants.name: binary-search-threshold
solution_variants.summary: Binary search the maximum tolerated penalty. During the check, scan only cells with penalty above the threshold and count how many blue runs must be painted, resetting at high-penalty red cells.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log A) time, O(1) extra memory.

tags.evidence: The objective is minimized by binary-searching the penalty threshold and checking feasibility.
tags.description: Binary search where each midpoint is an answer threshold and a separate monotone feasibility check decides whether the threshold is attainable.
tags.created_reason: The existing binary-search tag is broader; answer-threshold feasibility queries are a frequent and useful retrieval target.

tags.evidence: For a fixed threshold, greedily counting mandatory blue groups gives the minimum number of operations.
High-penalty red cells act as barriers that split the scan into independent segments.
tags.description: Split a one-dimensional scan into independent zones using forbidden or blocking positions, then solve or count each zone separately.
tags.created_reason: Existing tags cover greedy and binary search but not the reusable observation that hard blockers partition the decision space.
